I have the full file. Here is my line-by-line review.

pkg/cmd/session_tui.go

Line 29-33: `sessionTUIResult` returned via final model.
- (1) ✅ Clean result-extraction pattern; reading the result off the final model after `p.Run()` is the standard Bubble Tea idiom.

Line 38-42: `tuiMode` constants.
- (2) ✅ Good use of iota + a comment per value. Note `modeProjects` is overloaded to mean both "project browser" and "global search" (gated by `globalActive`); the routing in `Update` (266-276) handles it, but the mode name undersells what it covers.

Line 55-130: the `sessionTUI` struct.
- (3) ⚠️ This struct has ~50 fields spanning five sub-screens (list, search, browser, global search, preview, target). It's the strongest signal that the file should be split. The fields are grouped with comments, which helps, but state for each screen is interleaved into one model. Consider extracting per-screen sub-structs (e.g. `globalSearchState`, `browserState`) to make invariants local and reduce cross-screen coupling.

Line 79-81 / 81: `viewMode string // "dense" | "sparse"`.
- (4) ⚠️ Magic strings. `"dense"`/`"sparse"` recur at 552, 621-624, 926, 1843 and are persisted to config. Define `const viewDense, viewSparse = "dense", "sparse"` so a typo can't silently break the toggle.

Line 112-116: `searchSeq` / `snippetSeq` / `searchCancel`.
- (5) ✅ The seq-token debounce + `context.CancelFunc` to abort superseded FTS queries is well thought out and the comment explains the "why" (freeing a DB connection on a broad prefix query). Good.
- (6) ❓ `searchSeq` and `snippetSeq` are shared by both the project-scoped search (modeList) and the global search (modeProjects). That's safe only because exactly one search is ever active at a time. It works, but it's an implicit invariant worth a one-line comment.

Line 200-210: `Init`.
- (7) ✅ Correctly conditional on `globalSearching`; batches blink + pre-seeded query.

Line 212-284: `Update`.
- (8) ✅ Idiomatic top-level type switch; preview checked before mode-routing (260-263) with a clear "why" comment.
- (9) ⚠️ The `snippetResultMsg` handler (239-256) duplicates the same nil-map-init + copy loop for the global vs. filtered maps. Extract a helper `mergeSnippets(dst *map[string]string, src map[string]string)` to DRY the two branches.

Line 227-229: building a fresh `context.WithCancel` per debounce fire.
- (10) ✅ Stored on the model and cancelled on the next fire (224-226). Note the final in-flight ctx is never cancelled on TUI exit — minor leak, but the process is exiting so it's benign.

Line 291-320: `refreshAfterWarm`.
- (11) ✅ Solid: preserves cursor by session id, only refreshes in-place when home list is visible, re-runs settled cross-project search. Error path uses `slog.Debug` per conventions.

Line 324-334: `indexOfSession`.
- (12) ✅ Clear, single-purpose, good comment on the "return 0 on missing" choice.

Line 337-394: `updateList`.
- (13) ✅ Good key handling; the `r` (not `enter`) resume with the "stray return can't launch an agent" comment (373-375) is a nice safety rationale.
- (14) ✅ `case " ", "space"` defensively handles both forms.

Line 399-431: `updateSearch`.
- (15) ✅ The "arrows commit search and move the now-focused list" behavior (413-418) is a thoughtful UX touch, well commented.

Line 508-541: `moveCursorWithin` / `clampScrollWithin`.
- (16) ✅ Good DRY extraction of the three list cursors into shared pointer-based helpers. This is the right call given three near-identical list scrollers.

Line 559-570 / 469-476 / 1485-1492 / 1756-1767: the four `*Height` functions.
- (17) ⚠️ Four separate local `const chrome` values (5, 4, 9, 6) with differing derivations. They're commented, but the arithmetic is fragile and easy to drift from the actual `render*` output. At minimum name them distinctly or centralize the chrome accounting next to the renderers they mirror.
- (18) ⚠️ `projectsHeight` (chrome=9) is a real correctness smell: `renderProjects` injects a variable number of date-bucket header lines (1510-1513), but `projectsHeight` returns a fixed row budget that does NOT subtract those bucket separators. With several buckets visible, fewer project rows actually fit than the scroll math assumes, so the cursor can land on a row rendered below the fold. The other lists don't have this problem because they have no interspersed headers. This deserves a fix (account for buckets in the visible-window height) or at least a documented known-limitation.

Line 632-665: `applyFilter`.
- (19) ⚠️ This runs a SYNCHRONOUS `m.store.Search` (638) when a query is ready, whereas the whole `searchDebounceMsg`/`runSearch` machinery (1891-1912) exists specifically to keep FTS off the UI thread. `applyFilter` is invoked from `cycleAgent` (594) while a search is active, so pressing `a` during a 2-char search runs a blocking query on the UI thread — inconsistent with the async design and a potential hitch on a large index. Consider routing agent-cycle re-filtering through the async path too (as the global path already does at 588-592).

Line 657-664 / 1467-1473 / 1925-1931 / 1943-1948: cursor clamp-after-filter.
- (20) ⚠️ The `if cursor > len-1 { cursor = len-1 }; if cursor < 0 { cursor = 0 }; top = 0` block is copy-pasted in four places. Extract `clampCursorToLen(cursor, top *int, n int)`. This is the most repeated DRY violation in the file.

Line 697-702: doc comment on `minQueryLen`.
- (21) ⚠️ The comment's first line reads "ftsQuery turns free-form input into a safe FTS5 prefix query (alnum tokens only)." but it is attached to `const minQueryLen`, not to `ftsQuery` (whose real doc is at 719). This looks like a stray/duplicated first line — it mislabels the constant. Drop that first sentence so the comment documents `minQueryLen`.

Line 737-798: `ftsQuery` / `fieldTokens` / `segmentTokens`.
- (22) ✅ Excellent. The tokenizer-mirroring rationale (the index's unicode61 splitting, why `poem.txt` becomes adjacent tokens, why no FTS5 syntax char can reach MATCH) is exactly the kind of "why" comment the conventions ask for, and the injection-safety reasoning is valuable.

Line 802-808: style vars with raw color codes.
- (23) ❓ Palette numbers (`"240"`, `"213"`, `"255"`, plus `"232"`/`"221"` at 951 and the `colorForAgent` codes at 1323-1339) are scattered. Not wrong, but a small named palette block would make the theme adjustable in one place. Low priority.

Line 810-827: `View`.
- (24) ✅ Clean switch; mirrors the `Update` routing. `AltScreen = true` set correctly.

Line 921-942 / 1838-1853: `sessionRow` / `globalRow` width math.
- (25) ⚠️ Magic offsets: `m.lineWidth()-24`/`-26` (928), `-22`/`-24` (940), `-30`/`-32` (1844), `-46`/`-48` (1851). These encode gutter+agent+time+turns column widths but are uncommented bare numbers, and the title-vs-snippet pair differs by 2 each time. Pull the layout constants out (or compute from the rendered column widths) and comment what each represents; today changing the agent tag width (line 1067, `%-8s`) silently desyncs all four.
- (26) ✅ The year-stamp overflow shrink (934-940) with its "can't get pushed off the line and wrap" comment is a nice detail.
- (27) ⚠️ Minor: `when` is computed at 924 before the `sparse` branch but is unused in that branch (sparse recomputes `relativeTime` at 930). Harmless, but compute it inside the dense branch.

Line 946-989: `renderSnippet`.
- (28) ✅ The `\x02`/`\x03` highlight-mark stream parsing is correct and the column-clipping is handled. The control chars are magic but are an index/FTS contract; a one-line "these are the snippet match delimiters emitted by sessionindex" comment would help a future reader (the link to where they're produced isn't here).

Line 1014-1055: `renderPreview` / `renderTarget`.
- (29) ✅ Fine. `renderTarget`'s `label[len(a.provider.Name()):]` slice trick (1048) to re-style only the name prefix is slightly clever-for-clever's-sake but correct.

Line 1099-1122: `relativeTime`.
- (30) ✅ Good, including the prior-year disambiguation comment. Returns "" on parse error which callers pad safely.

Line 1151-1166: `sessionMarkdown`.
- (31) ✅ Reasonable fallback chain (real render → FTS body → placeholder). The nested `if err == nil` ladder (1152-1160) is a bit deep but readable.

Line 1174-1194: `openOrBuildResumeIndex`.
- (32) ✅ Good handling of missing/empty DB. `_ = s.Close()` (1187) on the empty-rebuild path is correct. Named returns document the `builtFresh` contract well.

Line 1206-1218: `startIndexWarm`.
- (33) ✅ Returns a cancel even on the `builtFresh`/error paths so callers can always `defer`/call it; the "must never block process exit" rationale is clear. Goroutine sends `indexWarmedMsg` via `p.Send`, which is the supported thread-safe path.

Line 1223-1320: `selectResumeViaTUI`.
- (34) ✅ Thorough: empty-index guard, installed-agent resolution, preset validation with a `ValidationError`, prefs persistence with `slog.Debug` on failure. The `final.(sessionTUI)` type assertion is checked (1290-1293).
- (35) ❓ `config.Load(nil)` error is swallowed (1272) — acceptable here (defaults are fine), but per conventions a `slog.Debug` would be consistent with how the rest of the function logs.

Line 1345-1354: `enterBrowser`.
- (36) ⚠️ `ListProjects` error is silently ignored and `projectsLoaded` is set true regardless (1350), so a transient failure yields a permanently empty browser with no retry and no log. Add `slog.Debug` and/or don't latch `projectsLoaded` on error.

Line 1370-1385: `drillInto`.
- (37) ⚠️ On `ListByProject` error it just `return`s with no state change and no log (1372-1374). To the user, pressing enter on a project does nothing with no feedback. At minimum `slog.Debug` the error.

Line 1454-1474: `applyProjectFilter`.
- (38) ✅ Simple substring filter; fine. (Shares the clamp-block DRY issue noted in #20.)

Line 1532-1559: `projectRow` / `agentCountChips`.
- (39) ✅ Deterministic ordering via `sort.Strings`. `32` (1539) is another bare width magic number (see #25).

Line 1592-1599: `dayDiff`.
- (40) ✅ Correct calendar-day diff using local midnight truncation; good comment.

Line 1603-1640 / 1642-1697: `updateGlobalSearch` / `updateGlobalResults`.
- (41) ✅ Behaviorally thorough (scope toggle, edit-search, resume/preview parity with the list).
- (42) ⚠️ The "esc quits at search root, else backs out" block is duplicated verbatim in both (1605-1611 and 1647-1653). Small, but it's exactly the kind of branch where the two copies can drift; a tiny helper would prevent that.

Line 1711-1733: `toggleGlobalScope`.
- (43) ❓ Calls `m.globalSelected()` twice (1716, 1718). Trivial; bind once.

Line 1769-1834: `renderGlobalResults`.
- (44) ✅ The footer-hint assembly is intricate but each branch is commented with its rationale (esc semantics, conditional "enter browse results"). Acceptable for a TUI footer.

Line 1865-1912: async search messages + `runSearch`.
- (45) ⚠️ `runSearch(seq int, kind tuiMode, ctx context.Context)` puts `ctx` last; Go convention is `ctx` as the first parameter. Cosmetic but a `go vet`/lint-adjacent nit.
- (46) ✅ Captures `store`, `query`, `projectID`, `fq` by value before returning the closure — correct to avoid racing the model. `searchDebounceDelay = 50ms` is a named const (good, not a magic literal).

Line 1953-1988: `requestVisibleSnippets`.
- (47) ✅ Defensive `append([]Session(nil), …)` copy of the visible rows before handing them to the goroutine (1966, 1977) — this is the right move to avoid the background snippet query reading a slice the UI loop later mutates. Good goroutine-safety hygiene.
- (48) ❓ Concurrency on `m.store`: the snippet/search closures call `store.SnippetsContext`/`SearchContext` from goroutines while the UI thread can call `store.Search` synchronously in `applyFilter` (638). This assumes the `sessionindex.Store` is safe for concurrent reads. Worth confirming the store opens with a connection pool / read-only WAL; if it's a single `*sql.DB`, it's fine, but it's an unstated dependency.

Overall structure / receivers
- (49) ✅ Value-receiver `Update`/`View` returning the model, pointer-receiver mutators called on addressable locals — consistent with Bubble Tea idioms and works correctly here.
- (50) ⚠️ Error handling across the file leans heavily on `_ =`/silent `err == nil` (lines 638, 1162, 1184, 1230, 1272, 1347, 1372). The conventions call for `log/slog` observability; the warm/refresh paths do this well, but the interactive paths (drillInto, enterBrowser) should match.

(a) Most important issues

1. projectsHeight scroll mismatch (#18, line 1485-1492): the fixed row budget ignores the variable date-bucket header lines that `renderProjects` actually emits, so the cursor can scroll onto a project row rendered below the visible fold. This is the one concrete correctness bug, not just style.

2. Synchronous FTS in applyFilter on the UI thread (#19, line 638): pressing `a` (cycle agent) during an active project-scoped search runs a blocking `store.Search`, defeating the carefully built async/debounced search path. Route it through the async path for consistency and to avoid a hitch on large indexes.

3. Pervasive DRY duplication of the cursor-clamp block (#20) and the snippet-merge/esc-quit branches (#9, #42), plus uncommented row-width magic offsets (#25): individually minor, collectively they're the main maintainability drag and the place where copies will silently drift.

(b) File size / splitting

Yes — at 1988 lines this file is too large and should be split. It cleanly factors into: model + Update/Init routing (session_tui.go), rendering (session_tui_view.go), the FTS query builder + async search machinery (session_tui_search.go), and the all-projects browser + global search (session_tui_browser.go). The sub-sections are already marked with `// ---- ... ----` banners, so the seams exist; splitting would also localize the per-screen state if combined with the sub-struct suggestion in #3. The code is well-commented and the abstractions (shared cursor helpers, shared `rowLabel`/`headerLeft`) show genuine DRY effort — the size is the main structural concern, not the quality.

Line 1711-1733: `toggleGlobalScope`.

Line 1769-1834: `renderGlobalResults`.

Line 1865-1912: async search messages + `runSearch`.
- (45) ⚠️ `runSearch(seq int, kind tuiMode, ctx context.Context)` puts `ctx` last; Go convention is `ctx` as the first parameter. Cosmetic but a `go vet`/lint-adjacent nit.
- (46) ✅ Captures `store`, `query`, `projectID`, `fq` by value before returning the closure — correct to avoid racing the model. `searchDebounceDelay = 50ms` is a named const (good, not a magic literal).

Line 1953-1988: `requestVisibleSnippets`.
- (47) ✅ Defensive `append([]Session(nil), …)` copy of the visible rows before handing them to the goroutine (1966, 1977) — this is the right move to avoid the background snippet query reading a slice the UI loop later mutates. Good goroutine-safety hygiene.
- (48) ❓ Concurrency on `m.store`: the snippet/search closures call `store.SnippetsContext`/`SearchContext` from goroutines while the UI thread can call `store.Search` synchronously in `applyFilter` (638). This assumes the `sessionindex.Store` is safe for concurrent reads. Worth confirming the store opens with a connection pool / read-only WAL; if it's a single `*sql.DB`, it's fine, but it's an unstated dependency.

Overall structure / receivers
- (49) ✅ Value-receiver `Update`/`View` returning the model, pointer-receiver mutators called on addressable locals — consistent with Bubble Tea idioms and works correctly here.
- (50) ⚠️ Error handling across the file leans heavily on `_ =`/silent `err == nil` (lines 638, 1162, 1184, 1230, 1272, 1347, 1372). The conventions call for `log/slog` observability; the warm/refresh paths do this well, but the interactive paths (drillInto, enterBrowser) should match.

(a) Most important issues

1. projectsHeight scroll mismatch (#18, line 1485-1492): the fixed row budget ignores the variable date-bucket header lines that `renderProjects` actually emits, so the cursor can scroll onto a project row rendered below the visible fold. This is the one concrete correctness bug, not just style.

2. Synchronous FTS in applyFilter on the UI thread (#19, line 638): pressing `a` (cycle agent) during an active project-scoped search runs a blocking `store.Search`, defeating the carefully built async/debounced search path. Route it through the async path for consistency and to avoid a hitch on large indexes.

3. Pervasive DRY duplication of the cursor-clamp block (#20) and the snippet-merge/esc-quit branches (#9, #42), plus uncommented row-width magic offsets (#25): individually minor, collectively they're the main maintainability drag and the place where copies will silently drift.

(b) File size / splitting

Yes — at 1988 lines this file is too large and should be split. It cleanly factors into: model + Update/Init routing (session_tui.go), rendering (session_tui_view.go), the FTS query builder + async search machinery (session_tui_search.go), and the all-projects browser + global search (session_tui_browser.go). The sub-sections are already marked with `// ---- ... ----` banners, so the seams exist; splitting would also localize the per-screen state if combined with the sub-struct suggestion in #3. The code is well-commented and the abstractions (shared cursor helpers, shared `rowLabel`/`headerLeft`) show genuine DRY effort — the size is the main structural concern, not the quality.</result>

# Code Review: PR #244 reconstruct.go / provider.go (codex, cursor, deepseek, droid, gemini)

## Context verified

- Shared `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go` already centralizes the heavy lifting: `FlattenSessionData`, `PrepareTurns`, `ResolveWorkspaceRoot`, synthetic-turn filtering, tool/thinking folding. Every provider's `ReconstructSession` correctly opens with `spi.PrepareTurns` + `spi.ResolveWorkspaceRoot`. Good — the genuinely shared policy lives in one place.
- `spi.ScanReporter.Add` is confirmed nil-safe (`pkg/spi/global.go:53`). Go version is 1.26 (`min` builtin available).

---

## pkg/providers/codexcli/reconstruct.go

Line 20 (new): `reconstructedCodexCliVersion = "0.139.0"`

- (1) ⚠️ Hard-coded native CLI version to make the loader accept the file. Reasonable per the comment, but it's a brittle magic constant that will silently rot; fine for now, flag for future maintenance.

Line 39-108 (new): `ReconstructSession`

- (2) ✅ The dual-emission (response_item + event_msg per turn) is well-justified by the comment (lines 30-34) and is the single most format-specific piece across all 5 — correctly not abstracted.
- (3) ✅ UUIDv7 with v4 fallback (lines 47-50) is correct and unique to Codex (its IDs are v7). Other providers correctly use plain `uuid.NewString()`.
- (4) ✅ Timestamp ordering is correct: meta at `base`, turns at `base+(i+1)s` (line 82), so records sort after meta.
- (5) ❓ `data.SessionID` is read directly at line 75 without nil guard — safe only because `PrepareTurns` already rejected `data == nil`. Same idiom in droid (line 61). Correct but relies on that invariant; fine.

Line 114-127 (new): `NativeSessionPath` — ✅ clean, dated path, documented why `projectPath` is unused.

### codexcli/provider.go (the large 259-line addition)

Line 364-405 (new): `GetAgentChatSessionByPath`

- (6) ✅ Good optimization with a clear "why" (avoids O(N²) reindex). The `debugRaw` branch preserving old behavior (lines 376-384) is a thoughtful correctness guard.

Line 623-640 (new): `readCodexJSONL(collectRaw)` refactor

- (7) ✅ Clean DRY refactor — two thin wrappers (`readSessionRawData`, `readSessionRecords`) over one parameterized reader. Good.

Line 1031-1100 (new): `scanCodexSessionHeader` + `userMessageMarker` substring screen

- (8) ✅ The cheap-substring-before-JSON-parse optimization is sound and well-commented (false positives only cost a wasted parse). Single-pass replaces two opens.
- (9) ⚠️ Line ~1100: `if h.firstUserMessage == ""` returns `(nil, nil)` to mean "empty session". A user message that is literally empty-after-trim is indistinguishable from "no user message," but that matches prior behavior, so acceptable.

Line 1160-1210 (new): `ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` (parallel header scan)

- (10) ⚠️ `workers := min(runtime.NumCPU(), 12); if workers < 1 { workers = 1 }` — the guard is dead code: `runtime.NumCPU()` is always ≥1, so `min(≥1, 12)` is always ≥1. Drop the guard.
- (11) ✅ Concurrency is correct: `refs` guarded by `mu`, `r.Add` is atomic+nil-safe, order-independence documented. Good.
- (12) ❓ Only Codex implements `ProgressEnumerator` / parallel scan. Justified (Codex sessions can be hundreds of MB; comment says so) — the asymmetry is intentional, not an oversight.

---

## pkg/providers/droidcli/reconstruct.go

Line 19-21: `droidTimestamp` — identical format string to codex/gemini (`2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z`).

- (13) ⚠️ See DRY verdict — this millis formatter is byte-identical in codex (line 24), droid (line 20), gemini (line 18). Three copies.

Line 41-44 (new): `title` from `data.Slug` else `"Resumed session"` — identical literal/logic to cursor (line 86-88) and deepseek (line 41-44). Three copies of the same default.

Line 51-64 (new): `session_start` header

- (14) ✅ Emits `specstorySourceSessionId` provenance (line 61) — matches codex.
- (15) ⚠️ `owner: os.Getenv("USER")` (line 56) can be empty in cron/CI/container contexts. Low risk but no fallback; worth a `slog.Debug` or accepting silently.

Line 66-92 (new): linear `parentId` chain

- (16) ✅ Correct: first message roots the thread (no parentId), subsequent chained. Role mapping at lines 69-72 is the same `assistant`-default / `user` pattern as deepseek and cursor.

---

## pkg/providers/deepseektui/reconstruct.go

Line 18-20: `deepseekTimestamp` — **micros** (`.000000Z`), the one timestamp helper that legitimately differs. Good that it's distinct.

Line 46-56 (new): message loop

- (17) ⚠️ Role mapping (lines 48-51) and `content: [{"type":"text","text":...}]` (line 54) are duplicated near-verbatim with droid (lines 69-80). Prime candidate for a shared helper.

## pkg/providers/geminicli/reconstruct.go

Line 18-20: `geminiTimestamp` — identical millis format to codex/droid (dup).

Line 45-59 (new): message loop — note Gemini's asymmetric shape (user content is `[{text}]`, gemini content is a bare string). Correctly format-specific.

- (19) ✅ Line 43: `projectHash, _ := HashProjectPath(cwd)` ignores error deliberately ("best-effort," comment present, resume keys off dir). Acceptable.
- (20) ❓ Line 79: `newID[:8]` — safe since `uuid.NewString()` is always 36 chars, but a raw slice with no length check is a latent footgun if the ID source ever changes. Minor.

Line 93-150 (new): `NativeSessionPath` + `prepareGeminiProjectDir`

- (21) ✅ The most complex resolver, and the only one that creates a `.project_root` marker. Logic (reuse existing dir, fall back to hash dir on basename collision) is sound and well-commented. This is genuinely provider-specific and correctly not shared.

## pkg/providers/cursorcli/reconstruct.go

This is the outlier — content-addressed SQLite Merkle-DAG, ~218 lines, almost nothing shareable with the other four. Reviewed in detail.

Line 31-49 (new): `blobID`, `pbField`, `pbRef`

- (23) ⚠️ Line 47: `raw, _ := hex.DecodeString(idHex)` swallows the error. Safe because every caller passes a `blobID()` output (valid hex), but a misuse would silently encode a truncated ref. A comment noting the invariant (or a panic on misuse) would harden it.
- (24) ✅ `pbField` correctly rejects nothing but documents `fieldNum < 16` (single tag byte). All call sites use fields 1-3. Fine.

Line 78-152 (new): `ReconstructSession`

- (25) ✅ The DAG construction (messages → metadata subtree → root → meta head) is carefully commented and the validation provenance ("validated against `cursor-agent --resume`") is recorded. Good.
- (26) ⚠️ `name` defaults to `"Resumed session"` (line 87) — fourth copy of that literal/fallback (cursor here uses `data.Slug` directly, droid/deepseek use `strings.TrimSpace(data.Slug)`). Inconsistent: cursor does **not** TrimSpace the slug; droid/deepseek do. A whitespace-only slug would slip through here and produce a blank title. Minor correctness drift.
- (27) ❓ No `specstorySourceSessionId` provenance (and the comment notes Cursor's meta blob can't easily carry extra fields). Consistent with the "Cursor stores nothing recoverable" theme but worsens the provenance inconsistency.

Line 164-200 (new): `buildStoreDB`

- (28) ✅ Building in a temp file (SQLite needs a handle) then reading bytes back is the pragmatic choice, with proper `defer` cleanup. Error handling closes the db on each failure path. Slightly verbose but correct.
- (29) ⚠️ Map-iteration insert order (line 185) makes the output bytes non-deterministic run-to-run. Irrelevant for correctness (content-addressed), but means the bytes aren't reproducible — worth a one-line note if reproducibility is ever desired.

### cursorcli/provider.go — `ListAllAgentChatSessions`

- (30) ✅ Well-documented why `OriginCwd` is empty (md5 one-way hash; recovered at reindex via NativePath). Consistent error-skip pattern with the other enumerators.

---

## (a) DRY verdict

The PR did the **important** DRY work already: flattening, turn preparation, workspace resolution, and synthetic-turn filtering all live in `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go` and every provider consumes them identically. That's the right altitude and it's done well.

What remains duplicated across the 5 is **small and format-adjacent**, but real:

1. **Identical millis timestamp formatter** — `t.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z")` is byte-for-byte identical in codex, droid, and gemini (deepseek legitimately differs with micros).
2. **`"Resumed session"` title default** — 4 copies (cursor, deepseek, droid; gemini/codex don't title), and they've **already drifted**: cursor doesn't `TrimSpace` the slug, droid/deepseek do (issue 26).
3. **Role-string mapping + text-content-part shape** — `role := "assistant"; if user { "user" }` plus `[]map{{"type":"text","text":...}}` is duplicated near-verbatim in deepseek and droid (and the role half in cursor).

Recommendation: hoist three tiny helpers into `pkg/spi` and let providers call them:

- `spi.ResumedSessionTitle(slug string) string` — trims and applies the `"Resumed session"` default, killing the drift in issue 26.
- `spi.RoleString(role string) string` — the user/assistant mapping (gemini's `gemini`/`user` and codex's dual labels stay local).
- A shared `spi.RFC3339Millis(t)` for the three identical formatters (keep deepseek's micros local).

Net savings are modest (~15-25 lines), but the real payoff is **consistency** — it removes the already-present slug-trim divergence and prevents future drift. I would **not** try to unify the serialization bodies; the native formats (JSONL dual-stream, indented JSON, parentId chain, protobuf/SQLite DAG) are too different and forcing them together would hurt readability. Cursor in particular should stay entirely standalone.

## (b) Three most important issues

1. **Inconsistent provenance tracking (correctness/feature gap).** Only codex and droid embed `specstorySourceSessionId`. deepseek, gemini, and cursor record no link back to the source session. If provenance/round-trip-origin matters (the PR is literally about portability), this asymmetry should be a deliberate, documented decision — right now it reads as an oversight. At minimum, deepseek and gemini have a natural metadata slot for it.
2. **Slug-default drift (issue 26).** cursor uses `data.Slug` without trimming while droid/deepseek trim before the `"Resumed session"` fallback — a whitespace-only slug yields a blank Cursor title. Fixed for free by the shared `ResumedSessionTitle` helper.
3. **Dead guard in codex parallel scan (issue 10).** `if workers < 1` after `min(runtime.NumCPU(), 12)` is unreachable. Cosmetic, but it's in the most complex new code path and should be removed.

## (c) Test-quality assessment

Overall **good and meaningful**, not tautological — four of five do a genuine round-trip (reconstruct → re-parse via the real forward `GenerateAgentSession`/`parseFactorySession` → re-flatten → assert turn-for-turn equality), which is exactly the right assertion for this feature and catches real serializer bugs.

Strengths:
- codex has both `_RoundTrip` and `_Structure` (asserts session_meta first, provenance, exactly-one user event, no synthesized environment_context) — the strongest coverage.
- gemini's `TestNativeSessionPath_CreatesProjectDir` actually exercises the `.project_root` creation with a mocked home — the one non-trivial resolver gets a real test.
- Fixtures deliberately include thinking + a tool call with Summary/FormattedMarkdown, so the flatten-folding path is covered.

Weaknesses:
- **Heavy copy-paste:** `strptr`, `reconstructSampleData`, and `TestReconstructSession_Empty` are near-identical across all 5 files (only the sample language/IDs change). Acceptable as per-package fixtures (Go discourages cross-package test helpers), but the `_Empty` test is truly identical 5×.
- **Cursor has no forward round-trip** — only `_Structure` (hand-rolled protobuf `refsOf` scanner). It never verifies the reconstructed `store.db` is actually readable by Cursor's own forward parser, unlike the other four. That's the weakest coverage of the set; if a cursor store→SessionData parser exists, the test should use it.
- **`opts.MigrationNote` is untested in all 5** — the leading-agent-turn injection path (a documented feature of `ReconstructOptions`) has zero coverage here. Worth one test case.
- **WorkspaceRoot fallback** (`opts.WorkspaceRoot==""` → `data.WorkspaceRoot`) is never asserted; all tests pass an explicit `WorkspaceRoot`.
- Not flaky: tests use `t.TempDir()` and mocked home dirs; no time/sleep/network dependence. The non-deterministic cursor byte output (issue 29) is not asserted, so no flakiness there.

Concrete test recommendation: add one `MigrationNote` round-trip assertion (shared shape across providers) and, for cursor, a forward-parse round-trip if the parser exists.

## Review: PR #244 — store.go, store_test.go, project_identity.go, project_identity_test.go

### pkg/sessionindex/store.go

**Connection pragmas vs. multi-connection reader (lines ~104–124)**

Line 104 (new): DSN sets `busy_timeout` + `journal_mode(WAL)`; lines 110–111 set max open/idle conns to `maxConns`; lines 113–124 run per-connection pragmas once via `db.Exec`.

- (1) ⚠️ This is the most important correctness issue. `synchronous`, `cache_size`, `temp_store`, `mmap_size`, `page_size` are **per-connection** SQLite settings. The loop runs them once via `db.Exec`, which applies them to whichever single pooled connection happens to serve that Exec. `pkg/provenance` gets away with this because it uses `SetMaxOpenConns(1)` (confirmed: provenance/store.go:36). But `OpenReader` here uses `readerConns = 4` — so reader connections 2–4 are created later by the pool **without** these pragmas (no `mmap_size`, default `cache_size`, etc.). The comment at lines 28–31 explicitly justifies multiple readers for performance, yet the performance pragmas silently don't reach most of them. The fix is to put these in the DSN as `_pragma=...` (so every new connection applies them) or use a connection init hook. Also note `page_size` is effectively a no-op after the DB/schema already exists (it requires setting before creation or a VACUUM).
- (2) ✅ DSN path is built with `filepath.ToSlash` and the path interpolated into a `file:` URI; values that reach SQL are bound parameters elsewhere. No injection surface here.
- (3) ❓ The DSN mixes pragma syntaxes: `busy_timeout=15000` (equals) vs `journal_mode(WAL)` (parens). Copied verbatim from provenance and modernc accepts both, so functionally fine — just visually inconsistent.

**ensureSchema migrations (lines 194–203 / diff 200,205,209)**

- (4) ⚠️ Three migration statements swallow all errors with `_, _ =`. For `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` that already exists this is the intended "ignore duplicate" path, but it also hides genuine failures (locked DB, I/O error), and there is **no slog at all** in this package, so a failed migration is completely invisible. A `slog.Debug` on non-nil error would preserve observability per the project's logging convention without changing behavior.
- (5) ⚠️ `ensureSchema` runs on **every** `Open` including `OpenReader`, performing DDL (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP INDEX`) through a handle documented (lines 98–100) as "Never write through this handle." In steady state these are no-ops, but on a fresh/old DB the read path mutates schema — a mild contract violation worth a one-line acknowledgement in the `OpenReader` doc, or guarding `ensureSchema` to the writer.
- (6) ✅ The composite index design (lines 178–184) and its why-comment are excellent — clearly explains serving both filter and sort order, and the migration that drops the superseded single-column index.

**Upsert path (lines ~257–393)**

- (7) ✅ Transaction handling in `UpsertBatch` is correct: `committed` flag + deferred rollback, prepared-statement closer deferred, and `prepareUpsert` cleans up its own partially-prepared statements on error before returning an empty closer (no double-close, no leak). Good.
- (8) ✅ `upsertOne` / `deleteOldFTSRow` manual FTS maintenance is non-obvious but thoroughly why-commented (lines 251–256, 280–284), including the `IsNew` fast-path and the legacy-NULL-rowid fallback. This is the strongest-documented part of the file.
- (9) ✅ The `INSERT OR REPLACE` column count matches the 17 placeholders (lines 323–324), and `sessionInsertColumns = sessionColumns + ", fts_rowid"` keeps insert/scan in lockstep with `scanSessions`. DRY and verified consistent.

**Query helpers (lines ~438–622)**

- (10) ✅ All `Query`/`QueryContext` call sites pair with `defer func(){ _ = rows.Close() }()` and check `rows.Err()` — no resource leaks. `Search`/`Snippets` use bound parameters (`MATCH ?`, parameterized `(agent = ? AND session_id = ?)` clauses); `prefixed()` builds column lists only from the compile-time `sessionColumns` constant. No SQL injection.
- (11) ⚠️ `ListProjects` (lines 471–476 / diff 477,482): `ps.ProjectName` is set at creation to `pname`, then re-checked `if ps.ProjectName == "" { ps.ProjectName = pname }` a few lines later — redundant (the second assignment can never fire with the same `pname`). Minor dead branch.
- (12) ❓ `ListProjects` final sort uses `sort.Slice` (line 491 / diff 497), which is not stable — projects sharing an identical `LastActivity` get a nondeterministic order between calls. Probably immaterial for a picker, but `sort.SliceStable` would make the UI deterministic.
- (13) ✅ The `Search` `ORDER BY s.updated_at DESC LIMIT 500` decision (recency over BM25) is given an unusually good why-comment (diff 546–551). Same for the lazy `Snippets` split.
- (14) ❓ `Search` passes the raw user `query` straight to `sessions_fts MATCH ?`. That's injection-safe, but a syntactically-invalid FTS5 query (stray quote, bare operator) returns a SQL error to the caller rather than empty results. Fine if the interactive layer sanitizes/recovers — worth confirming that's handled upstream, since this is the live keystroke path.

### pkg/utils/project_identity.go

- (15) ✅ `findGitRoot` (diff 1128–1143), `resolveGitConfigPath` (1149–1185), worktree `.git`-file + `commondir` handling are correct and well-commented, and the worktree path is actually exercised by a test. Path handling does `filepath.Abs` then `Clean`. Solid.
- (16) ⚠️ Dead production code: after this change, `EnsureProjectIdentity` resolves everything via `resolveIdentity`, so `generateGitID()` (line 211) and `generateProjectName()` (line 381) are **no longer called by any production code** — only by tests (confirmed via grep: call sites are all in `_test.go`). `resolveIdentity` reimplements their core (read origin → normalize → hash; repo name) rather than reusing them, because those two don't handle the worktree `.git`-file case. Net result is two now-orphaned methods kept alive solely by their own tests. Recommend either deleting them (and the tests that only exist to cover them) or routing them through the new shared helpers. This is a DRY/dead-code flag the project conventions call out explicitly.
- (17) ✅ `readOriginURL` + package-level `originRemoteURLRegex` (diff 1107–1121) correctly de-duplicate the previously-copied regex that lived in both `generateGitID` and `generateProjectName`. Good DRY win — that part of the refactor is clean.
- (18) ✅ Case-fold logic: `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` as a `var` (line 196) with an honest why-comment about Windows-on-a-branch, and `canonicalizeWorkspacePath` (203–208) applied only to the **path-based workspace_id**, never to `git_id` (which hashes the normalized URL). Correct scoping — case folding a URL hash would be wrong, and it isn't.
- (19) ❓ `ComputeProjectID` non-git fallback (diff 1236–1242): it prefers `persistedWorkspaceID(cwd)` over the freshly-canonicalized hash. The comment is honest that this means projects with a pre-canonicalization `.project.json` keep their old (possibly case-divergent) id — i.e. the case-fold fix (18) does **not** retroactively help any project that already has a `.project.json`. That's a defensible "all consumers agree on one value" tradeoff and it's documented, but it does narrow the bug fix to genuinely-new/portable sessions only. Worth confirming that's the intended blast radius.
- (20) ✅ `resolveIdentity` reusing a throwaway `&ProjectIdentityManager{projectRoot: root}` to borrow the stateless hashing/normalization helpers (diff 1198–1207) is a reasonable, clearly-commented way to avoid duplicating that logic.
- (21) ✅ `ComputeProjectID` uses a guard clause for empty cwd and otherwise has effectively a single resolution flow with early returns by precedence (git_id → persisted → computed) — readable, matches the "guard clauses OK" convention.

### Test-quality assessment

**store_test.go**

- (22) ✅ Real behavior, not tautologies: `TestSearchFTS` and `TestSearchAndSnippets` exercise the actual FTS index (body vs name match, project scoping, prefix `oauth*`, snippet STX/ETX markers). `TestUpsertIsIdempotent` verifies the replace actually purges stale FTS content. `TestUpsertHandlesLegacyNullRowid` is the standout — it forces the `fts_rowid = NULL` migration state via a direct `UPDATE` and proves both the body-read fallback and the delete-by-key fallback, then asserts `fts_rowid` is repopulated. That's exactly the kind of edge the conventions ask for.
- (23) ⚠️ Gaps: no test for `UpsertBatch` with multiple rows or its rollback-on-error path (the transaction is the riskiest code and only the single-row `Upsert` wrapper is exercised); nothing covers `OpenReader` / the multi-connection reader (which is where issue (1) lives); no `SearchContext` cancellation test. White-box reaches into `s.db` are fine (same package).
- (24) ✅ Not flaky, no real sleeps/time deps, each uses `t.TempDir()` and `t.Cleanup`. Helpers (`newSession`, `mustUpsert`, `openTemp`) keep them DRY. Reasonable that these aren't table-driven — each asserts a distinct behavior rather than varying inputs over one function.

**project_identity_test.go**

- (25) ✅ `TestComputeProjectID` is effectively the executable spec: subtests for monorepo-subdir collapse, two-clone SSH/HTTPS convergence, worktree `.git`-file resolution (with a hand-built `commondir`), remote-less collapse + distinct-repo separation, no-git stability, persisted-vs-computed precedence, git_id-wins, and empty-cwd error. Strong, real-logic coverage that maps directly to the new code paths.
- (26) ✅ `TestCanonicalizeWorkspacePath` / `TestWorkspaceIDCaseFolding` mutate the `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` var with `defer` restore and assert both OS behaviors deterministically — the right way to test an OS-conditional without being flaky. (Implication: these and any future case tests must not run `t.Parallel()`, since they share that global — currently fine.)

### Most important issues

1. **(1) Per-connection pragmas don't reach the extra reader connections.** The `synchronous`/`cache_size`/`temp_store`/`mmap_size` pragmas are set once via `db.Exec`, a pattern lifted from provenance's `SetMaxOpenConns(1)` world, but `OpenReader` uses 4 connections — so 3 of them silently run with defaults, defeating the stated reason for opening multiple readers. Move these into the DSN as `_pragma=` or a connection init hook.
2. **(16) `generateGitID` and `generateProjectName` are now dead in production** — called only by their own tests after `resolveIdentity` took over, and `resolveIdentity` reimplements rather than reuses them. Delete or unify per the DRY/no-dead-code conventions.
3. **(4)/(5) No logging and read-path DDL.** The package has zero `slog` despite swallowing migration errors with `_, _ =`, and `ensureSchema` performs schema writes on the documented read-only `OpenReader` handle. Add a debug log on swallowed migration errors and reconcile the OpenReader "never write" contract.

Everything else is minor (redundant `ProjectName` re-check, `sort.Slice` non-stability, FTS query-validation responsibility). The FTS/transaction/resource-management core is correct and unusually well-commented; the test suites are high quality, with the main gap being `UpsertBatch`/rollback and the multi-connection reader.

# Code Review: PR #244 reconstruct.go / provider.go (codex, cursor, deepseek, droid, gemini)

## Context verified

- Shared `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go` already centralizes the heavy lifting: `FlattenSessionData`, `PrepareTurns`, `ResolveWorkspaceRoot`, synthetic-turn filtering, tool/thinking folding. Every provider's `ReconstructSession` correctly opens with `spi.PrepareTurns` + `spi.ResolveWorkspaceRoot`. Good — the genuinely shared policy lives in one place.
- `spi.ScanReporter.Add` is confirmed nil-safe (`pkg/spi/global.go:53`). Go version is 1.26 (`min` builtin available).

---

## pkg/providers/codexcli/reconstruct.go

Line 20 (new): `reconstructedCodexCliVersion = "0.139.0"`

- (1) ⚠️ Hard-coded native CLI version to make the loader accept the file. Reasonable per the comment, but it's a brittle magic constant that will silently rot; fine for now, flag for future maintenance.

Line 39-108 (new): `ReconstructSession`

- (2) ✅ The dual-emission (response_item + event_msg per turn) is well-justified by the comment (lines 30-34) and is the single most format-specific piece across all 5 — correctly not abstracted.
- (3) ✅ UUIDv7 with v4 fallback (lines 47-50) is correct and unique to Codex (its IDs are v7). Other providers correctly use plain `uuid.NewString()`.
- (4) ✅ Timestamp ordering is correct: meta at `base`, turns at `base+(i+1)s` (line 82), so records sort after meta.
- (5) ❓ `data.SessionID` is read directly at line 75 without nil guard — safe only because `PrepareTurns` already rejected `data == nil`. Same idiom in droid (line 61). Correct but relies on that invariant; fine.

Line 114-127 (new): `NativeSessionPath` — ✅ clean, dated path, documented why `projectPath` is unused.

### codexcli/provider.go (the large 259-line addition)

Line 364-405 (new): `GetAgentChatSessionByPath`

- (6) ✅ Good optimization with a clear "why" (avoids O(N²) reindex). The `debugRaw` branch preserving old behavior (lines 376-384) is a thoughtful correctness guard.

Line 623-640 (new): `readCodexJSONL(collectRaw)` refactor

- (7) ✅ Clean DRY refactor — two thin wrappers (`readSessionRawData`, `readSessionRecords`) over one parameterized reader. Good.

Line 1031-1100 (new): `scanCodexSessionHeader` + `userMessageMarker` substring screen

- (8) ✅ The cheap-substring-before-JSON-parse optimization is sound and well-commented (false positives only cost a wasted parse). Single-pass replaces two opens.
- (9) ⚠️ Line ~1100: `if h.firstUserMessage == ""` returns `(nil, nil)` to mean "empty session". A user message that is literally empty-after-trim is indistinguishable from "no user message," but that matches prior behavior, so acceptable.

Line 1160-1210 (new): `ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` (parallel header scan)

- (10) ⚠️ `workers := min(runtime.NumCPU(), 12); if workers &lt; 1 { workers = 1 }` — the guard is dead code: `runtime.NumCPU()` is always ≥1, so `min(≥1, 12)` is always ≥1. Drop the guard.
- (11) ✅ Concurrency is correct: `refs` guarded by `mu`, `r.Add` is atomic+nil-safe, order-independence documented. Good.
- (12) ❓ Only Codex implements `ProgressEnumerator` / parallel scan. Justified (Codex sessions can be hundreds of MB; comment says so) — the asymmetry is intentional, not an oversight.

---

## pkg/providers/droidcli/reconstruct.go

Line 19-21: `droidTimestamp` — identical format string to codex/gemini (`2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z`).

- (13) ⚠️ See DRY verdict — this millis formatter is byte-identical in codex (line 24), droid (line 20), gemini (line 18). Three copies.

Line 41-44 (new): `title` from `data.Slug` else `"Resumed session"` — identical literal/logic to cursor (line 86-88) and deepseek (line 41-44). Three copies of the same default.

Line 51-64 (new): `session_start` header

- (14) ✅ Emits `specstorySourceSessionId` provenance (line 61) — matches codex.
- (15) ⚠️ `owner: os.Getenv("USER")` (line 56) can be empty in cron/CI/container contexts. Low risk but no fallback; worth a `slog.Debug` or accepting silently.

Line 66-92 (new): linear `parentId` chain

- (16) ✅ Correct: first message roots the thread (no parentId), subsequent chained. Role mapping at lines 69-72 is the same `assistant`-default / `user` pattern as deepseek and cursor.

---

## pkg/providers/deepseektui/reconstruct.go

Line 18-20: `deepseekTimestamp` — **micros** (`.000000Z`), the one timestamp helper that legitimately differs. Good that it's distinct.

Line 46-56 (new): message loop

- (17) ⚠️ Role mapping (lines 48-51) and `content: [{"type":"text","text":...}]` (line 54) are duplicated near-verbatim with droid (lines 69-80). Prime candidate for a shared helper.
- (18) ❓ No `specstorySourceSessionId` provenance written anywhere in the deepseek session (neither metadata nor top-level), unlike codex/droid. See issue (b).

Line 58-73: ✅ static metadata block is fine; `message_count`/`total_tokens`/`model` placeholders are reasonable.

## pkg/providers/geminicli/reconstruct.go

Line 18-20: `geminiTimestamp` — identical millis format to codex/droid (dup).

Line 45-59 (new): message loop — note Gemini's asymmetric shape (user content is `[{text}]`, gemini content is a bare string). Correctly format-specific.

- (19) ✅ Line 43: `projectHash, _ := HashProjectPath(cwd)` ignores error deliberately ("best-effort," comment present, resume keys off dir). Acceptable.
- (20) ❓ Line 79: `newID[:8]` — safe since `uuid.NewString()` is always 36 chars, but a raw slice with no length check is a latent footgun if the ID source ever changes. Minor.

Line 93-150 (new): `NativeSessionPath` + `prepareGeminiProjectDir`

- (21) ✅ The most complex resolver, and the only one that creates a `.project_root` marker. Logic (reuse existing dir, fall back to hash dir on basename collision) is sound and well-commented. This is genuinely provider-specific and correctly not shared.
- (22) ⚠️ No provenance / source-session id stored for Gemini either (top-level session object has none).

## pkg/providers/cursorcli/reconstruct.go

This is the outlier — content-addressed SQLite Merkle-DAG, ~218 lines, almost nothing shareable with the other four. Reviewed in detail.

Line 31-49 (new): `blobID`, `pbField`, `pbRef`

- (23) ⚠️ Line 47: `raw, _ := hex.DecodeString(idHex)` swallows the error. Safe because every caller passes a `blobID()` output (valid hex), but a misuse would silently encode a truncated ref. A comment noting the invariant (or a panic on misuse) would harden it.
- (24) ✅ `pbField` correctly rejects nothing but documents `fieldNum &lt; 16` (single tag byte). All call sites use fields 1-3. Fine.

Line 78-152 (new): `ReconstructSession`

- (25) ✅ The DAG construction (messages → metadata subtree → root → meta head) is carefully commented and the validation provenance ("validated against `cursor-agent --resume`") is recorded. Good.
- (26) ⚠️ `name` defaults to `"Resumed session"` (line 87) — fourth copy of that literal/fallback (cursor here uses `data.Slug` directly, droid/deepseek use `strings.TrimSpace(data.Slug)`). Inconsistent: cursor does **not** TrimSpace the slug; droid/deepseek do. A whitespace-only slug would slip through here and produce a blank title. Minor correctness drift.
- (27) ❓ No `specstorySourceSessionId` provenance (and the comment notes Cursor's meta blob can't easily carry extra fields). Consistent with the "Cursor stores nothing recoverable" theme but worsens the provenance inconsistency.

Line 164-200 (new): `buildStoreDB`

- (28) ✅ Building in a temp file (SQLite needs a handle) then reading bytes back is the pragmatic choice, with proper `defer` cleanup. Error handling closes the db on each failure path. Slightly verbose but correct.
- (29) ⚠️ Map-iteration insert order (line 185) makes the output bytes non-deterministic run-to-run. Irrelevant for correctness (content-addressed), but means the bytes aren't reproducible — worth a one-line note if reproducibility is ever desired.

### cursorcli/provider.go — `ListAllAgentChatSessions`

- (30) ✅ Well-documented why `OriginCwd` is empty (md5 one-way hash; recovered at reindex via NativePath). Consistent error-skip pattern with the other enumerators.

---

## (a) DRY verdict

The PR did the **important** DRY work already: flattening, turn preparation, workspace resolution, and synthetic-turn filtering all live in `pkg/spi/reconstruct.go` and every provider consumes them identically. That's the right altitude and it's done well.

What remains duplicated across the 5 is **small and format-adjacent**, but real:

1. **Identical millis timestamp formatter** — `t.Format("2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z")` is byte-for-byte identical in codex, droid, and gemini (deepseek legitimately differs with micros).
2. **`"Resumed session"` title default** — 4 copies (cursor, deepseek, droid; gemini/codex don't title), and they've **already drifted**: cursor doesn't `TrimSpace` the slug, droid/deepseek do (issue 26).
3. **Role-string mapping + text-content-part shape** — `role := "assistant"; if user { "user" }` plus `[]map{{"type":"text","text":...}}` is duplicated near-verbatim in deepseek and droid (and the role half in cursor).

Recommendation: hoist three tiny helpers into `pkg/spi` and let providers call them:

- `spi.ResumedSessionTitle(slug string) string` — trims and applies the `"Resumed session"` default, killing the drift in issue 26.
- `spi.RoleString(role string) string` — the user/assistant mapping (gemini's `gemini`/`user` and codex's dual labels stay local).
- A shared `spi.RFC3339Millis(t)` for the three identical formatters (keep deepseek's micros local).

Net savings are modest (~15-25 lines), but the real payoff is **consistency** — it removes the already-present slug-trim divergence and prevents future drift. I would **not** try to unify the serialization bodies; the native formats (JSONL dual-stream, indented JSON, parentId chain, protobuf/SQLite DAG) are too different and forcing them together would hurt readability. Cursor in particular should stay entirely standalone.

## (b) Three most important issues

1. **Inconsistent provenance tracking (correctness/feature gap).** Only codex and droid embed `specstorySourceSessionId`. deepseek, gemini, and cursor record no link back to the source session. If provenance/round-trip-origin matters (the PR is literally about portability), this asymmetry should be a deliberate, documented decision — right now it reads as an oversight. At minimum, deepseek and gemini have a natural metadata slot for it.
2. **Slug-default drift (issue 26).** cursor uses `data.Slug` without trimming while droid/deepseek trim before the `"Resumed session"` fallback — a whitespace-only slug yields a blank Cursor title. Fixed for free by the shared `ResumedSessionTitle` helper.
3. **Dead guard in codex parallel scan (issue 10).** `if workers &lt; 1` after `min(runtime.NumCPU(), 12)` is unreachable. Cosmetic, but it's in the most complex new code path and should be removed.

## (c) Test-quality assessment

Overall **good and meaningful**, not tautological — four of five do a genuine round-trip (reconstruct → re-parse via the real forward `GenerateAgentSession`/`parseFactorySession` → re-flatten → assert turn-for-turn equality), which is exactly the right assertion for this feature and catches real serializer bugs.

Strengths:
- codex has both `_RoundTrip` and `_Structure` (asserts session_meta first, provenance, exactly-one user event, no synthesized environment_context) — the strongest coverage.
- gemini's `TestNativeSessionPath_CreatesProjectDir` actually exercises the `.project_root` creation with a mocked home — the one non-trivial resolver gets a real test.
- Fixtures deliberately include thinking + a tool call with Summary/FormattedMarkdown, so the flatten-folding path is covered.

Weaknesses:
- **Heavy copy-paste:** `strptr`, `reconstructSampleData`, and `TestReconstructSession_Empty` are near-identical across all 5 files (only the sample language/IDs change). Acceptable as per-package fixtures (Go discourages cross-package test helpers), but the `_Empty` test is truly identical 5×.
- **Cursor has no forward round-trip** — only `_Structure` (hand-rolled protobuf `refsOf` scanner). It never verifies the reconstructed `store.db` is actually readable by Cursor's own forward parser, unlike the other four. That's the weakest coverage of the set; if a cursor store→SessionData parser exists, the test should use it.
- **`opts.MigrationNote` is untested in all 5** — the leading-agent-turn injection path (a documented feature of `ReconstructOptions`) has zero coverage here. Worth one test case.
- **WorkspaceRoot fallback** (`opts.WorkspaceRoot==""` → `data.WorkspaceRoot`) is never asserted; all tests pass an explicit `WorkspaceRoot`.
- Not flaky: tests use `t.TempDir()` and mocked home dirs; no time/sleep/network dependence. The non-deterministic cursor byte output (issue 29) is not asserted, so no flakiness there.

Concrete test recommendation: add one `MigrationNote` round-trip assertion (shared shape across providers) and, for cursor, a forward-parse round-trip if the parser exists.</result>

- (3) ❓ The DSN mixes pragma syntaxes: `busy_timeout=15000` (equals) vs `journal_mode(WAL)` (parens). Copied verbatim from provenance and modernc accepts both, so functionally fine — just visually inconsistent.

**ensureSchema migrations (lines 194–203 / diff 200,205,209)**

- (4) ⚠️ Three migration statements swallow all errors with `_, _ =`. For `ALTER TABLE ... ADD COLUMN` that already exists this is the intended "ignore duplicate" path, but it also hides genuine failures (locked DB, I/O error), and there is **no slog at all** in this package, so a failed migration is completely invisible. A `slog.Debug` on non-nil error would preserve observability per the project's logging convention without changing behavior.
- (5) ⚠️ `ensureSchema` runs on **every** `Open` including `OpenReader`, performing DDL (`CREATE`, `ALTER`, `DROP INDEX`) through a handle documented (lines 98–100) as "Never write through this handle." In steady state these are no-ops, but on a fresh/old DB the read path mutates schema — a mild contract violation worth a one-line acknowledgement in the `OpenReader` doc, or guarding `ensureSchema` to the writer.
- (6) ✅ The composite index design (lines 178–184) and its why-comment are excellent — clearly explains serving both filter and sort order, and the migration that drops the superseded single-column index.

**Upsert path (lines ~257–393)**

- (7) ✅ Transaction handling in `UpsertBatch` is correct: `committed` flag + deferred rollback, prepared-statement closer deferred, and `prepareUpsert` cleans up its own partially-prepared statements on error before returning an empty closer (no double-close, no leak). Good.
- (8) ✅ `upsertOne` / `deleteOldFTSRow` manual FTS maintenance is non-obvious but thoroughly why-commented (lines 251–256, 280–284), including the `IsNew` fast-path and the legacy-NULL-rowid fallback. This is the strongest-documented part of the file.
- (9) ✅ The `INSERT OR REPLACE` column count matches the 17 placeholders (lines 323–324), and `sessionInsertColumns = sessionColumns + ", fts_rowid"` keeps insert/scan in lockstep with `scanSessions`. DRY and verified consistent.

**Query helpers (lines ~438–622)**

- (10) ✅ All `Query`/`QueryContext` call sites pair with `defer func(){ _ = rows.Close() }()` and check `rows.Err()` — no resource leaks. `Search`/`Snippets` use bound parameters (`MATCH ?`, parameterized `(agent = ? AND session_id = ?)` clauses); `prefixed()` builds column lists only from the compile-time `sessionColumns` constant. No SQL injection.
- (11) ⚠️ `ListProjects` (lines 471–476 / diff 477,482): `ps.ProjectName` is set at creation to `pname`, then re-checked `if ps.ProjectName == "" { ps.ProjectName = pname }` a few lines later — redundant (the second assignment can never fire with the same `pname`). Minor dead branch.
- (12) ❓ `ListProjects` final sort uses `sort.Slice` (line 491 / diff 497), which is not stable — projects sharing an identical `LastActivity` get a nondeterministic order between calls. Probably immaterial for a picker, but `sort.SliceStable` would make the UI deterministic.
- (13) ✅ The `Search` `ORDER BY s.updated_at DESC LIMIT 500` decision (recency over BM25) is given an unusually good why-comment (diff 546–551). Same for the lazy `Snippets` split.
- (14) ❓ `Search` passes the raw user `query` straight to `sessions_fts MATCH ?`. That's injection-safe, but a syntactically-invalid FTS5 query (stray quote, bare operator) returns a SQL error to the caller rather than empty results. Fine if the interactive layer sanitizes/recovers — worth confirming that's handled upstream, since this is the live keystroke path.

### pkg/utils/project_identity.go

- (15) ✅ `findGitRoot` (diff 1128–1143), `resolveGitConfigPath` (1149–1185), worktree `.git`-file + `commondir` handling are correct and well-commented, and the worktree path is actually exercised by a test. Path handling does `filepath.Abs` then `Clean`. Solid.
- (16) ⚠️ Dead production code: after this change, `EnsureProjectIdentity` resolves everything via `resolveIdentity`, so `generateGitID()` (line 211) and `generateProjectName()` (line 381) are **no longer called by any production code** — only by tests (confirmed via grep: call sites are all in `_test.go`). `resolveIdentity` reimplements their core (read origin → normalize → hash; repo name) rather than reusing them, because those two don't handle the worktree `.git`-file case. Net result is two now-orphaned methods kept alive solely by their own tests. Recommend either deleting them (and the tests that only exist to cover them) or routing them through the new shared helpers. This is a DRY/dead-code flag the project conventions call out explicitly.
- (17) ✅ `readOriginURL` + package-level `originRemoteURLRegex` (diff 1107–1121) correctly de-duplicate the previously-copied regex that lived in both `generateGitID` and `generateProjectName`. Good DRY win — that part of the refactor is clean.
- (18) ✅ Case-fold logic: `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` as a `var` (line 196) with an honest why-comment about Windows-on-a-branch, and `canonicalizeWorkspacePath` (203–208) applied only to the **path-based workspace_id**, never to `git_id` (which hashes the normalized URL). Correct scoping — case folding a URL hash would be wrong, and it isn't.
- (19) ❓ `ComputeProjectID` non-git fallback (diff 1236–1242): it prefers `persistedWorkspaceID(cwd)` over the freshly-canonicalized hash. The comment is honest that this means projects with a pre-canonicalization `.project.json` keep their old (possibly case-divergent) id — i.e. the case-fold fix (18) does **not** retroactively help any project that already has a `.project.json`. That's a defensible "all consumers agree on one value" tradeoff and it's documented, but it does narrow the bug fix to genuinely-new/portable sessions only. Worth confirming that's the intended blast radius.
- (20) ✅ `resolveIdentity` reusing a throwaway `&amp;ProjectIdentityManager{projectRoot: root}` to borrow the stateless hashing/normalization helpers (diff 1198–1207) is a reasonable, clearly-commented way to avoid duplicating that logic.
- (21) ✅ `ComputeProjectID` uses a guard clause for empty cwd and otherwise has effectively a single resolution flow with early returns by precedence (git_id → persisted → computed) — readable, matches the "guard clauses OK" convention.

### Test-quality assessment

**store_test.go**

- (22) ✅ Real behavior, not tautologies: `TestSearchFTS` and `TestSearchAndSnippets` exercise the actual FTS index (body vs name match, project scoping, prefix `oauth*`, snippet STX/ETX markers). `TestUpsertIsIdempotent` verifies the replace actually purges stale FTS content. `TestUpsertHandlesLegacyNullRowid` is the standout — it forces the `fts_rowid = NULL` migration state via a direct `UPDATE` and proves both the body-read fallback and the delete-by-key fallback, then asserts `fts_rowid` is repopulated. That's exactly the kind of edge the conventions ask for.
- (23) ⚠️ Gaps: no test for `UpsertBatch` with multiple rows or its rollback-on-error path (the transaction is the riskiest code and only the single-row `Upsert` wrapper is exercised); nothing covers `OpenReader` / the multi-connection reader (which is where issue (1) lives); no `SearchContext` cancellation test. White-box reaches into `s.db` are fine (same package).
- (24) ✅ Not flaky, no real sleeps/time deps, each uses `t.TempDir()` and `t.Cleanup`. Helpers (`newSession`, `mustUpsert`, `openTemp`) keep them DRY. Reasonable that these aren't table-driven — each asserts a distinct behavior rather than varying inputs over one function.

**project_identity_test.go**

- (25) ✅ `TestComputeProjectID` is effectively the executable spec: subtests for monorepo-subdir collapse, two-clone SSH/HTTPS convergence, worktree `.git`-file resolution (with a hand-built `commondir`), remote-less collapse + distinct-repo separation, no-git stability, persisted-vs-computed precedence, git_id-wins, and empty-cwd error. Strong, real-logic coverage that maps directly to the new code paths.
- (26) ✅ `TestCanonicalizeWorkspacePath` / `TestWorkspaceIDCaseFolding` mutate the `caseInsensitiveFilesystem` var with `defer` restore and assert both OS behaviors deterministically — the right way to test an OS-conditional without being flaky. (Implication: these and any future case tests must not run `t.Parallel()`, since they share that global — currently fine.)

### Most important issues

1. **(1) Per-connection pragmas don't reach the extra reader connections.** The `synchronous`/`cache_size`/`temp_store`/`mmap_size` pragmas are set once via `db.Exec`, a pattern lifted from provenance's `SetMaxOpenConns(1)` world, but `OpenReader` uses 4 connections — so 3 of them silently run with defaults, defeating the stated reason for opening multiple readers. Move these into the DSN as `_pragma=` or a connection init hook.
2. **(16) `generateGitID` and `generateProjectName` are now dead in production** — called only by their own tests after `resolveIdentity` took over, and `resolveIdentity` reimplements rather than reuses them. Delete or unify per the DRY/no-dead-code conventions.
3. **(4)/(5) No logging and read-path DDL.** The package has zero `slog` despite swallowing migration errors with `_, _ =`, and `ensureSchema` performs schema writes on the documented read-only `OpenReader` handle. Add a debug log on swallowed migration errors and reconcile the OpenReader "never write" contract.

Everything else is minor (redundant `ProjectName` re-check, `sort.Slice` non-stability, FTS query-validation responsibility). The FTS/transaction/resource-management core is correct and unusually well-commented; the test suites are high quality, with the main gap being `UpsertBatch`/rollback and the multi-connection reader.</result>

The old code would have treated it as non-root and it could still be reached as a child only via string match, so in practice the reachable set is the same, but a malformed-parentUuid root is now lost. Very unlikely in real Claude data; worth a one-line note that non-string/non-nil parentUuid is intentionally ignored.

---

**(a) Most important issues**

1. **Cross-provider duplication of the parallel enumeration boilerplate** (provider.go 461-528 vs. codexcli 1139-1208). The walk + worker-pool + ref-collection scaffold is copied near-verbatim across providers, differing only in root dir, scan fn, and ref mapping. Given the repo's explicit DRY directive, extract a `spi` helper (e.g. `ScanInParallel(root, scanFn, r)`); this will only get copied again as more providers gain `ProgressEnumerator`. (obs 29)

2. **`scanClaudeSession` early-exit now gated on `cwd != ""`** (provider.go line 378). Correctness is fine (EOF terminates), but any session lacking a top-level `cwd` now reads to EOF on every scan, silently losing the early-out. Add a brief comment, or treat cwd as best-effort for exit purposes. (obs 21)

3. **`buildDAGs` parentUuid branch drops non-string/non-nil parents** (jsonl_parser.go 33-37). A record whose `parentUuid` is a non-string non-nil JSON value is now neither root nor child and vanishes from all DAGs. Extremely unlikely in real data, but a one-line comment documenting the intentional ignore would close the gap. (obs 3)

**(b) Test-quality assessment**

Strong overall. The reconstruct round-trip test (obs 49) is exemplary — it exercises the real forward parser rather than re-asserting the writer's own output, so it catches chain/ordering/escaping regressions end-to-end. The chain, native-path-encoding, and empty-guard tests each pin a distinct invariant with no tautology. Two soft spots: the DAG performance test is a wall-clock tripwire (inherently flake-prone, but well-mitigated by ~100x headroom and an explanatory comment, obs 9), and `TestIsSyntheticMessage_SlashCommandNoise` uses paired loops instead of the repo-preferred `want bool` table (obs 35). No tautological or trivial tests.

**(c) Logic duplicated across providers that should be shared**

- **The `ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` walk+parallel-scan pattern** — claudecode and codexcli are near-identical; belongs in `spi`. (obs 29)
- **The reconstruction timestamp format** `"2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z"` — codex wraps it in `codexTimestamp`, claude inlines the same literal; a shared `spi` formatter would unify them. (obs 43)
- **The `"[Request interrupted by user"` literal** appears in both `claudecode.isSyntheticMessage` and `spi.extraSyntheticTurnMarkers` — should reference one constant. (obs 18)
- Positively: the *core* reconstruction policy (`PrepareTurns`, `FlattenSessionData`, `ResolveWorkspaceRoot`, `SyntheticCommandTags`) is already correctly centralized in `spi` and consumed identically by both providers — that part of the DRY design is well executed. (obs 39)

**pkg/providers/claudecode/jsonl_parser.go**

Line 18-39 (modified): single-pass index building `recordByUuid` + `childrenByParent` adjacency map, with O(N²)→O(N) rationale comment.
- (1) ✅ Correct and well-motivated refactor. The comment explains *why* (the previous per-node rescan was O(N²)), which is exactly the "why not how" convention.
- (2) ✅ Line 29-31: skipping records with no uuid is correct — such a record can't be a traversal target. Matches the old behavior where un-keyed records were never inserted into `recordByUuid`.
- (3) ⚠️ Line 33-37: the branch structure is subtly different from before. Old code: a record is a root iff `parentUuid == nil`. New code: `if parentUuid is string → child; else if parentUuid == nil → root`. A record whose `parentUuid` exists but is a *non-string, non-nil* JSON value (e.g. a number, or the literal `false`) now falls through and is neither a child nor a root — it is silently dropped from all DAGs. The old code would have treated it as non-root and it could still be reached as a child only via string match, so in practice the reachable set is the same, but a malformed-parentUuid root is now lost. Very unlikely in real Claude data; worth a one-line note that non-string/non-nil parentUuid is intentionally ignored.

Line 41-50 (new): hoisting the per-child timestamp sort out of the traversal into a one-time pass over `childrenByParent`.
- (4) ✅ Correct equivalence: children are sorted once per parent list, preserving the same deterministic timestamp order the per-node sort produced, now O(N log N) total. Good comment.
- (5) ✅ Iterating `for parentUuid := range childrenByParent` then re-fetching the slice and `sort.Slice` mutates the slice in the map in place — correct (slices are reference-backed; no write-back needed).

Line 64-100 (modified): `buildDAGFromRoot` now takes `childrenByParent` and drops the inner rescan + orphan check.
- (6) ✅ The old "check if this child exists in our map (to handle orphans)" guard is correctly obsolete: `childrenByParent` is built only from records that already passed the `uuid` check at line 28, so every child is a real indexed node. No orphan can enter the adjacency map.
- (7) ✅ `visited` cycle-guard retained — important because this is now adjacency-driven; a parentUuid cycle would otherwise infinitely recurse. Good that it's preserved.

**pkg/providers/claudecode/jsonl_parser_test.go**

Line 110-127 (modified): bumped 10000→30000 records, comment updated to "300 records each".
- (8) ✅ Raising N is the right move: an O(N²) regression only trips a wall-clock ceiling once N is large, and the comment at line 136-141 explains this explicitly. Good.

Line 136-142 (modified): tightened ceiling 20s→3s with a detailed tripwire rationale.
- (9) ⚠️ This remains a wall-clock performance assertion — inherently the most flake-prone test style on shared CI. The 3s ceiling vs. "tens of ms" actual is ~100x headroom, which is a reasonable margin, and the comment justifies it well. Acceptable, but flag that any wall-clock tripwire can flake under heavy CI contention; the large headroom is the mitigation.

**pkg/providers/claudecode/path_utils.go**

Line 15 (new): `projectDirNameRegex` package-level compiled once.
- (10) ✅ Good — hoisting the regex to a package var (compiled once) is better than the old per-call `regexp.MustCompile` inside `GetClaudeCodeProjectDir`. DRY win.

Line 20-26 (new): `encodeProjectDirName`.
- (11) ✅ Encoding logic is byte-identical to the original inline code (replace `[^a-zA-Z0-9-]`→`-`, ensure leading dash), now shared by both call sites. This is the core path-encoding correctness point and it matches Claude Code's lossy scheme. Note the encoding is intentionally irreversible (consecutive specials collapse to multiple dashes, e.g. `My Projects(1)`→`My-Projects-1-`), which is why decode is done via the in-file `cwd` field elsewhere rather than by reversing the dir name — consistent with the comment in provider.go line 456-458.
- (12) ❓ Edge: a path containing a literal Unicode/non-ASCII char (e.g. `/Users/sé/app`) — the regex `[^a-zA-Z0-9-]` operates on bytes via Go's regexp on the string, so multi-byte runes become multiple dashes. This matches Claude Code's own JS behavior only if Claude also replaces per-UTF-16-unit; minor and pre-existing (not introduced here), but since the prompt asked to scrutinize encoding: the reconstructed dir could mismatch Claude's actual dir for non-ASCII cwds. Pre-existing behavior, not a regression.

Line 33-55 (new): `resolveClaudeProjectDir` — symlink-resolving, does NOT require dir to exist.
- (13) ✅ The distinction from `GetClaudeCodeProjectDir` is real and well-documented: this one tolerates a non-existent project dir (needed because the reconstruct caller creates it) and falls back to the raw path on `EvalSymlinks` error instead of erroring. Correct for the write-side use case.
- (14) ⚠️ Some duplication with `GetClaudeCodeProjectDir` (homedir fetch, empty→Getwd, EvalSymlinks, encode, join). The two differ only in error-vs-fallback on symlink failure and the existence check. Could be unified with a boolean, but the divergent error semantics arguably justify two readable functions over one branchy one. Minor; current form is acceptable per "simple/readable."
- (15) ✅ `%v` vs `%w` in error wrapping (line 36/43) matches the existing file's style (the other functions use `%v`), so it's locally consistent even though `%w` is generally preferred.

Line 114-116 (modified): `GetClaudeCodeProjectDir` now calls `encodeProjectDirName`.
- (16) ✅ Clean DRY substitution; the worked example in the comment (line 115) is preserved and accurate.

- (17) ✅ Good reuse of `spi.SyntheticCommandTags` as the single source of truth (confirmed shared with `spi.isSyntheticTurnText`). The comment correctly explains the Contains-vs-prefix distinction: warmup/`&lt;TEXTBLOCK&gt;` can be embedded → Contains; command/interrupt markers replace the prompt → prefix.
- (18) ✅ Line 268: hardcoded `"[Request interrupted by user"` prefix (no trailing `]`) deliberately catches the "…for tool use]" variant. Tested. Minor source-of-truth drift risk; could reference one constant.

Line 283-291 (new): `claudeSessionScan` struct.

Line 295-392 (new): `scanClaudeSession` (extracted from `extractSessionMetadata`).
- (20) ✅ Line 323-329: capturing `cwd` from the first record carrying one is the key new input for the restore index. Correct guard `cwd != ""`.
- (21) ⚠️ Line 378: the single-exit condition now requires `scan.cwd != ""` in addition to the prior three. If a session legitimately never carries a `cwd` (older Claude format, or a sidechain-only prefix), the loop no longer early-exits and reads the whole file every time. Correctness is fine (EOF terminates), but the early-out optimization silently degrades to full-file reads for any cwd-less session. Given Claude stamps cwd on every conversational record this is rare; acceptable, but worth a comment that cwd-absence defeats the early exit.
- (22) ✅ Line 340-348: the timestamp-capture comment correctly explains why it can't gate on "first real record" (the leading `{mode,sessionId,type}` record has no timestamp). Good "why" comment catching a real bug class.

- (25) ✅ Idiomatic and minimal. Returns trimmed inner or "". Good small helper. (Generic enough it could live in utils, but local scope is fine.)

Line 426-447 (modified): `extractSessionMetadata` now delegates to `scanClaudeSession`.
- (26) ✅ Clean thin wrapper; preserves the warmup-only `(nil, nil)` contract. Slug/Name now computed inline at the return — readable.

Line 451-453 (new): `ListAllAgentChatSessions` no-progress wrapper.
- (27) ✅ Matches the established codex pattern exactly. In use via reindex.go line 197.

Line 461-528 (new): `ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` — walk + parallel header scan.
- (28) ✅ Two-phase walk-then-scan, skip-unreadable-entries, nil-safe `r.Add(1)`, parallel workers — all correct and matches `ProgressEnumerator`.
- (29) ⚠️ **DRY (cross-provider):** this function is near-identical to `codexcli.ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` (lines 1139-1208). This is a strong candidate to extract into `pkg/spi` as something like `ScanInParallel(root string, scan func(path string) (*GlobalSessionRef, error), r *ScanReporter)`. - (30) ✅ Line 486-489: `min(runtime.NumCPU(), 12)` is fine on Go 1.26 (builtin `min`). The `if workers &lt; 1` guard is effectively dead (`NumCPU()` is always ≥1) but it's defensive and matches codex — harmless.
- (31) ❓ Line 491: `var refs []spi.GlobalSessionRef` (nil slice) here vs. codex's `make([]spi.GlobalSessionRef, 0, len(paths))`. Trivial inconsistency; preallocating would avoid a few growth reallocations under the lock. Cosmetic.
- (32) ✅ Line 462-465: missing projects dir → empty slice, not error. Correct (a machine with no Claude history shouldn't fail reindex).
- (33) ✅ Line 501 uses `slog.Warn` for scan failures while codex uses `slog.Debug` for the same condition — slight inconsistency in level, but Warn is arguably the better choice for a skipped session. Non-blocking; just note the divergence.

---

**(a) Most important issues**

1. **Cross-provider duplication of the parallel enumeration boilerplate** (provider.go 461-528 vs. codexcli 1139-1208). The walk + worker-pool + ref-collection scaffold is copied near-verbatim across providers, differing only in root dir, scan fn, and ref mapping. Given the repo's explicit DRY directive, extract a `spi` helper (e.g. `ScanInParallel(root, scanFn, r)`); this will only get copied again as more providers gain `ProgressEnumerator`. (obs 29)

2. **`scanClaudeSession` early-exit now gated on `cwd != ""`** (provider.go line 378). Correctness is fine (EOF terminates), but any session lacking a top-level `cwd` now reads to EOF on every scan, silently losing the early-out. Add a brief comment, or treat cwd as best-effort for exit purposes. (obs 21)

3. **`buildDAGs` parentUuid branch drops non-string/non-nil parents** (jsonl_parser.go 33-37). A record whose `parentUuid` is a non-string non-nil JSON value is now neither root nor child and vanishes from all DAGs. Extremely unlikely in real data, but a one-line comment documenting the intentional ignore would close the gap. (obs 3)

**(b) Test-quality assessment**

Strong overall. The reconstruct round-trip test (obs 49) is exemplary — it exercises the real forward parser rather than re-asserting the writer's own output, so it catches chain/ordering/escaping regressions end-to-end. The chain, native-path-encoding, and empty-guard tests each pin a distinct invariant with no tautology. Two soft spots: the DAG performance test is a wall-clock tripwire (inherently flake-prone, but well-mitigated by ~100x headroom and an explanatory comment, obs 9), and `TestIsSyntheticMessage_SlashCommandNoise` uses paired loops instead of the repo-preferred `want bool` table (obs 35). No tautological or trivial tests.

**(c) Logic duplicated across providers that should be shared**

- **The `ListAllAgentChatSessionsProgress` walk+parallel-scan pattern** — claudecode and codexcli are near-identical; belongs in `spi`. (obs 29)
- **The reconstruction timestamp format** `"2006-01-02T15:04:05.000Z"` — codex wraps it in `codexTimestamp`, claude inlines the same literal; a shared `spi` formatter would unify them. (obs 43)
- **The `"[Request interrupted by user"` literal** appears in both `claudecode.isSyntheticMessage` and `spi.extraSyntheticTurnMarkers` — should reference one constant. (obs 18)
- Positively: the *core* reconstruction policy (`PrepareTurns`, `FlattenSessionData`, `ResolveWorkspaceRoot`, `SyntheticCommandTags`) is already correctly centralized in `spi` and consumed identically by both providers — that part of the DRY design is well executed. (obs 39)</result>

The TUI lets the user toggle view mode with `v` (session_tui.go:621-624), so a toggle made during `search` is **not persisted**, while the same toggle during `resume` is. Should be `rm.viewMode`.

Line 191-194 (new): `liveIndex := NewLiveIndexer(cwd)` + defer Close.

- (1) ✅ `cwd` is in scope; nil-safe; placement and comment match the run/sync additions.

- (3) ✅ The watch callback runs concurrently across provider watchers; `Record` serializes on `LiveIndexer.mu` (reindex.go:459), so this is safe. The receiver-level mutex makes the multi-provider case correct without extra locking here.
